resume:
GRACE OKONKWO
Director of Manufacturing Operations

PROFILE
Manufacturing leader with 15 years in discrete and process manufacturing, specializing in plant turnarounds, OEE improvement, and multi-site standardization. I have led plants through lean transformations and capital expansions.

EXPERIENCE

Director of Manufacturing — Halcyon Industrial Products (2020–present)
Own three plants (Ohio, Tennessee, Mexico) producing injection-molded and assembled consumer-durables components. Lead a team of 3 plant managers and roughly 600 employees across the network.
- Led a network-wide OEE improvement program. Raised average OEE from 61% to 79% over two years.
- Note in later bullet: "Drove OEE from the high 60s to the low 80s" — figures cited inconsistently across reviews.
- Consolidated two product lines from the Ohio plant into Tennessee, closing the Ohio molding cell.
- Headcount: led 600 employees (summary) / "directly accountable for ~850 across the three sites" (per a separate section below).
- Capital: delivered a $12M automation expansion in Tennessee on time.

Plant Manager — Halcyon Industrial Products, Ohio (2016–2020)
- Ran the Ohio plant, ~250 employees.
- Reduced scrap rate from 7.2% to 3.1%.
- Joined Halcyon in 2017 (per this section) — though the title block above shows the Ohio role beginning in 2016.

Operations Manager — Veritas Molding (2010–2016)
- Managed two shifts of injection molding.
- Improved on-time delivery to 96%.

ADDITIONAL SCOPE NOTE
At Halcyon I have been directly accountable for approximately 850 employees across the three sites and a combined operating budget I usually describe as around $140M, though our network revenue is closer to $90M.

EDUCATION & CERTS
B.S. Industrial Engineering. Lean Six Sigma Black Belt. APICS CPIM.

SKILLS
OEE/TPM, lean transformation, multi-site standardization, capital project delivery, S&OP, plant consolidation, labor relations.
jd: Meridian Manufacturing Group is hiring a Director of Manufacturing to lead a three-plant network (combined ~700 employees) producing precision components for industrial customers. Reporting to the COO, you will own network OEE, the consolidated manufacturing budget, capital project delivery, and a multi-site standardization agenda. We are in the middle of a difficult plant consolidation and need a leader who has personally executed a similar move and can speak precisely about the decision, the execution, and — critically — the measurable outcome. In the interview, expect deep questions on a single transformation you led: we will probe the trigger, your specific role, the resistance you managed, and the hard numbers before and after. Consistency and command of your own facts matter; we will notice if figures shift. Black Belt and capital-delivery track record expected.

history.q: Grace, lay out your current scope at Halcyon — sites, team size, what you own.
I want to anchor on numbers. Your summary says 600 employees but a later section says about 850. Which is it, and why the gap?
Same question on budget — you mention a $140M operating budget but $90M network revenue. Help me reconcile that.
Let's go to the OEE program. What was the baseline and what triggered the program?
You cite 61% to 79% in one place and 'high 60s to low 80s' in another. Which figure is the real one?
Walk me through the Ohio-to-Tennessee consolidation. What drove the decision to close the Ohio molding cell?
What was your specific role in that consolidation versus the plant managers'?
How did you handle the affected Ohio workforce — what did you actually do for them?
What resistance did you hit from the Tennessee side absorbing the volume, and how did you manage it?
When did you actually join Halcyon? The title block says 2016 for Ohio but the role section says 2017.
Take the consolidation end to end. Give me the situation, your task, the actions you personally drove, and the measured result — throughput, cost, quality, whatever you tracked.

candidate_last_answer: Sure. So the situation was that Ohio was sub-scale — we had two molding lines running at maybe 55% utilization while Tennessee had open capacity and newer presses. My task as director was to consolidate without disrupting customer supply during the move. So what I did personally: I built the transfer plan, sequenced the tool moves so we never had both the Ohio and Tennessee version of a part down at the same time, I negotiated the temporary safety-stock build with our top three customers so we had a buffer, and I personally ran the weekly war-room through the whole cutover. I also owned the WARN-notice timing and the severance framework with HR for the Ohio team. We sequenced 40-some tools over about four months without a single line-down event for a customer.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: without a single line-down event for a customer

expected-yield: the missing Result quantification beyond the avoided-disruption claim — the actual cost takeout, utilization gain at Tennessee, or unit-cost delta from consolidating the two lines, with a number and timeframe
